blocks.text: Chapter 5.
Dances with Foes: Debates, Negotiation, and Motivational Interviewing
The ideas in 30 seconds

The best debaters and negotiators win not by piling up arguments but by finding common ground, leading with a few strong points, and asking questions — which is how champion debater Harish Natarajan out-persuaded IBM's Project Debater, an AI with access to 400 million articles.

Neil Rackham's research on expert negotiators found they plan around shared interests, present fewer reasons, ask questions more than twice as often as average negotiators, and avoid defend–attack spirals.
Argument dilution means every weak reason you add undermines your strongest one: it hands the other side an easy point to knock down and signals that your case needs padding.

Motivational interviewing — open-ended questions, reflective listening, and affirming a person's autonomy — helps people talk themselves into change, because people are far more persuaded by reasons they voice than by reasons they're given.

Listening is itself a form of influence: Arnaud Gagneur's single maternity-ward conversations measurably raised childhood vaccination rates, and Betty Bigombe's patient listening brought warlord Joseph Kony to peace talks.
How One Human Out-Argued a Supercomputer

In February 2019, in front of a large San Francisco audience, IBM's Project Debater — an AI trained on some 400 million newspaper articles — faced Harish Natarajan, who holds the record for most international debate competition victories. The motion: preschool should be subsidized. The computer argued for; Harish argued against, with fifteen minutes to prepare. The audience voted before and after, and the winner was whoever moved more minds. Harish won decisively.

The machine did what most of us do in an argument: it buried the opponent in facts, statistics, and reasons, treating persuasion as a war of attrition. Harish did the opposite. He opened by acknowledging common ground — preschool can genuinely benefit children, and he wasn't disputing that. Then he reframed the actual question: not whether preschool is good, but whether subsidies are the best use of scarce funds, noting they often help families who least need the help.

Instead of contesting every one of the computer's points, he conceded the reasonable ones and concentrated his fire on one or two decisive weaknesses. Conceding a valid point doesn't make you look weak; it makes you look credible and fair-minded, which makes your remaining arguments land harder.

Grant's larger lesson: a good debate is not a war, and it isn't even a tug-of-war you can win by pulling harder. It's more like an unchoreographed dance with a partner who has a different set of steps in mind. If you try to lead too forcefully, the other person resists. If you adapt — finding rhythm in points of agreement, stepping back where they have a point — you can move together somewhere new. Approaching disagreement in prosecutor mode (chapter 1) triggers defensiveness; approaching it like a curious scientist invites the other side to rethink with you.
What Expert Negotiators Actually Do Differently

Researcher Neil Rackham studied what separates elite negotiators from merely experienced ones. He identified experts by track record — rated effective by both sides, with strong success rates and few deals that later blew up — then recorded and analyzed over a hundred live negotiations. Four differences stood out.

First, experts planned differently. Average negotiators prepared for battle, scripting arguments and anchoring on positions. Experts spent far more of their planning time searching for possible common ground — devoting roughly a third of their preparation to areas where the two sides might agree — and considered a wider range of options rather than one fixed path.

Second, experts made fewer arguments. Average negotiators assumed more reasons meant a stronger case; experts knew about argument dilution. A single compelling reason is stronger than that reason plus three mediocre ones, because a savvy counterpart attacks the weakest link and the whole case starts to feel flimsy. Quality beats quantity.

Third, experts asked far more questions. In Rackham's data, questions made up over a fifth of expert negotiators' comments — more than double the rate of average negotiators, who mostly asserted and rebutted. Questions like "So you don't see any merit in this proposal at all?" express curiosity, surface the other side's real interests, and invite them to reconsider without being told to.

Fourth, experts avoided defend–attack spirals — the escalating loop where each side dismisses the other's position and hardens its own. Rather than counterpunching, experts got curious about the disagreement. They also commented openly on their own feelings and on the process itself ("I'm confused by that reaction — can you help me understand it?"), which defused tension instead of feeding it, and they steered clear of irritating self-praise like calling their own offer "generous."
The through-line: persuasion improves when you hold your position a little more loosely and hold the relationship a little more tightly. Fewer, better reasons; more, better questions.
Motivational Interviewing: Helping People Persuade Themselves

When someone resists change, our instinct is the righting reflex — the urge to fix, correct, and lecture. It backfires: pressure to change typically strengthens resistance, because people defend their autonomy. Motivational interviewing (MI), developed by psychologists William Miller (from his work with problem drinkers) and Stephen Rollnick, starts from the opposite premise: we can rarely motivate someone else to change, but we can help them find their own motivation.

MI rests on three core practices. Ask open-ended questions that invite genuine reflection rather than yes/no defenses ("What would vaccinating look like for you?" rather than "Don't you know vaccines are safe?"). Practice reflective listening — restating what you heard, testing your understanding, and showing you grasp their concerns before offering anything. And affirm the person's desire and ability to change, making clear the decision is theirs.

The engine of MI is the distinction between change talk and sustain talk. Sustain talk is commentary defending the status quo; change talk is any hint of desire, ability, reasons, or need to change. Skilled interviewers don't argue with sustain talk — they acknowledge it — and when change talk appears, they ask elaborating questions: why would you want that, how might you do it? Hearing yourself articulate reasons to change is far more convincing than hearing someone else's reasons, because your own words don't threaten your autonomy. A good MI conversation ends with a summary of what you heard, a question about what you missed or got wrong, and a question about the person's plans and possible next steps.

MI has been validated in hundreds of controlled trials across smoking, addiction, gambling, diet and exercise, treatment adherence, and even prejudice reduction and coaching. One caveat is ethical: MI is a mirror, not a lever. It works only with a genuine desire to help people reach their own goals; used to manipulate someone toward yours, it stops being motivational interviewing and starts being coercion in disguise.
The Vaccine Whisperer's Twenty-Minute Conversations

Quebec neonatologist Arnaud Gagneur turned MI into public-health practice. Grant tells the story of Marie-Hélène Étienne-Rousseau, a mother firmly opposed to vaccination whose premature son, Tobie, spent his first months in the hospital. Rather than reciting evidence at her, a counselor from Gagneur's team asked open questions about her fears, listened reflectively, acknowledged the flood of conflicting information parents face, and closed by affirming that whatever she decided, he respected her judgment as a parent. Before leaving the hospital, she had Tobie vaccinated — and she later cited that feeling of being heard, not pressured, as what made the difference.

The approach scaled. In Gagneur's first study, a single MI-style conversation with mothers in the maternity ward raised their intention to vaccinate their children by about 15 percent. In a subsequent randomized trial across maternity wards, one such session made children roughly 9 percentage points more likely to be fully vaccinated two years later — a remarkable return on a conversation lasting well under an hour. The program earned Gagneur the nickname "the vaccine whisperer" and was expanded across Quebec.

The mechanism matters as much as the numbers. The counselors never told mothers they were wrong and never withheld information — when parents asked for facts, they provided them. But the facts came after understanding, inside a relationship of respect, and always alongside an explicit acknowledgment of the parent's freedom to choose. Persuasion that honors autonomy doesn't just avoid backfiring; it opens the door that lecturing slams shut.
Listening as Influence: Betty Bigombe in the Warlord's Camp

The hardest test of these ideas is a hostile counterpart. In the early 1990s, Ugandan minister Betty Bigombe was tasked with ending Joseph Kony's brutal Lord's Resistance Army insurgency. Where the military had failed with force, she began by listening: she spent months visiting camps and villages, asking rebels and civilians about their grievances and offering to hear Kony's side rather than condemn it. She pushed for amnesty for defectors and arranged for traditional cleansing ceremonies so former fighters could return home.

The listening worked where firepower hadn't. Kony — who took to calling her "Mummy" — eventually agreed to meet and entered peace talks, declaring a willingness to end the conflict. The negotiations ultimately collapsed, not because listening failed but because the government short-circuited it: President Museveni issued a seven-day ultimatum for the rebels to surrender, and the war reignited. Bigombe, who was named Uganda's Woman of the Year for the effort, demonstrated that even a violent extremist can be moved toward the table by someone genuinely trying to understand him.

Research backs up what Bigombe practiced. Studies by Guy Itzchakov and Avraham Kluger show that when people talk to attentive, nonjudgmental listeners, they become less anxious and less defensive, explore their own views more deeply, and end up with more complex, less extreme attitudes — they rethink on their own. Grant calls this magnetic quality inverse charisma: instead of dazzling people with what you say, you draw them out by how you listen. Listening well signals that you're interested in their interests, and it models the openness you hope they'll show your ideas. The best way into a closed mind is often through its owner's own voice.
In practice
Before your next negotiation or disagreement, spend a third of your prep listing points of possible agreement — and open the conversation by naming them.
Cut your case to your one or two strongest arguments; delete the supporting reasons that are merely okay, because they dilute the ones that matter.
Track your question ratio in a hard conversation: aim for at least one genuine question in every five comments, and get curious ("Help me understand...") instead of counterattacking.

When someone resists a change you want, suppress the righting reflex: ask an open-ended question about their concerns, reflect back what you hear, and ask an elaborating question the moment you hear any change talk.
End persuasion attempts by summarizing the other person's view, asking what you missed, and explicitly affirming that the choice is theirs.
If a discussion turns into a defend–attack spiral, name the process out loud ("I'm frustrated, and I don't think we're hearing each other — can we reset?") rather than escalating.
